entry.title: New Claude Code Hijacks Mouse Clicks in Your Terminal? Kill It with CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS
entry.content:
Note
Claude Code now captures mouse events inside the terminal to give you click-to-expand, hover, scroll acceleration, and so on — but that also means you can’t select text or click links with the mouse anymore. To turn it off, there’s no settings.json field and no CLI flag — only environment variables. The one I reach for the most is CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 (v2.1.195+), which kills clicks / drag / hover but keeps the scroll wheel.

Intro

After the recent Claude Code update, the terminal now supports clicking things inside it — Yes, No, that kind of stuff. The problem is, as a developer I usually have multiple projects open, and every time I switch back to a project I end up clicking Yes by accident — when I really didn’t want to run the thing!

The whole experience is pretty rough, so I figured I’d document how to fix it.

Why is my mouse suddenly getting hijacked?

Basically, in v2.1.195 Claude Code added mouse interactions inside the terminal — drag, click, hover, all of it. Previous versions didn’t have this.

The feature itself is fine I think, but the thing is, as a developer I’m constantly bouncing between N projects, and I live inside the VS Code integrated terminal. So every time I switch back to a project, my mouse click lands on YES by accident and runs something I didn’t want to run. Pretty bad experience.

And imagine Claude Code asks me whether to run:

and I accidentally click YES, and now stuff is gone that I didn’t want gone. That’s actually kind of serious.

The behavior is controlled by the environment variable CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS. This is the “kill click / drag / hover, keep the scroll wheel” version — for me personally this is enough.

If you don’t even want the scroll wheel hijacked, use the other one instead:

There’s currently no /config menu or settings.json field for this, so environment variables are the only way.

Adding it to .zshrc

I’m on zsh so I’ll only walk through zsh here, but if you’re on bash or fish it’s basically the same idea.

Open ~/.zshrc and add this at the bottom:

Save it, then either:

Note
An already-running claude session won’t pick this up automatically — you have to quit and restart Claude Code for the new env var to take effect.

bash users edit ~/.bashrc instead, fish users add set -gx CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS 1 to ~/.config/fish/config.fish — same idea.

Don’t want to touch config? Three bypass hotkeys

If you don’t want to edit .zshrc and you just need to select some text occasionally, every terminal has a one-off bypass key for the mouse capture:

TerminalHotkey
iTerm2Hold Option while dragging
macOS TerminalHold Fn while dragging
VS Code / Cursor integrated terminalHold Shift while dragging
Other terminalsUsually Shift while dragging too

VS Code also has a setting terminal.integrated.macOptionClickForcesSelection: true, which makes Option + click directly force the cursor position — that’s a VS Code-specific setting, nothing to do with Claude Code itself.

Wrap up

Adding mouse interaction to Claude Code is a nice idea in theory, but for people who constantly switch projects and like selecting text with the mouse, it’s honestly pretty annoying.

The simplest fix is to drop CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 into your ~/.zshrc. Scroll wheel stays, clicks and drags stop getting hijacked, and you stop accidentally hitting YES and firing off commands you didn’t want.
entry.summary: When you're working in Claude Code and try to select text in the terminal or click a link in an error message, the clicks get hijacked by Claude Code itself. This post walks through the difference between CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE and CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS, how to add it to your .zshrc, and the one-off bypass hotkeys for iTerm2, macOS Terminal, and VS Code when you don't want to touch your config.

entry.title: design.md: If Your AI-Generated UI Keeps Drifting, You're Missing Google's design.md
entry.content:
Note
design.md is a format spec released by Google Labs that splits a design system into two layers — “YAML design Tokens” and “Markdown design rationale” — so that AI Coding tools like Claude Code and Cursor see more than just hex codes and font sizes; they actually understand the design intent behind them. The built-in CLI handles lint (including WCAG contrast checks), diff, and export to Tailwind or W3C DTCG. It’s currently in alpha.

Intro

Have you ever done some Vibe Coding and noticed the AI’s layout is always a little off, and no matter how many times you say “the spacing is wrong here, the colour is inconsistent there”, it still produces a slightly broken screen? If yes, it’s because you haven’t given the AI the rules of your design system properly.

To solve exactly this, this post walks through design.md, an AI design system spec released by Google Labs. The goal is to let AI Coding tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot actually read your design system and produce consistent UI, so you don’t have to keep repeating “wrong colour, wrong font size” over and over.

Note
If you’re not familiar yet with rules files for AI like CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md, take a look at CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI: Writing Tips That Make AI Understand Your Project and Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP first — it’ll make a lot more sense why design.md exists.

Where design.md came from: incubated from Stitch

As always, let’s do a quick history detour first.

design.md actually comes out of Stitch, Google’s AI design tool (first shown at Google I/O 2025). Stitch is a UI design tool powered by Google Gemini — you give it a text Prompt or upload a sketch, and it quickly generates a UI screen (frontend code included).

So when it launched, it was honestly pretty wild.

Then somewhere around 2026, Google released the design.md format itself under Apache 2.0 on GitHub (google-labs-code/design.md).

What does that mean? It basically means they’re trying to “standardise” Stitch’s design system rules.

After all, the most common pain point for Vibe Coding developers is inconsistent UI and inconsistent style. But if you open-source this design spec, the business value gets pretty huge — whether you use Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, or any other AI Coding tool, you can use this spec to produce consistent UI.

Wait, you’re wondering what business value open-sourcing brings?

A few examples:

  • Becoming the industry standard means owning the definition. Think of it like OpenAPI or Kubernetes back in the day — when they were first open-sourced they looked harmless, but once everyone started using them, Google’s voice became the loudest about where the spec should go, what fields to add, what Token types to support. Because they’re the one in charge.
  • Other AI tools voluntarily become allies. Once Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot start marketing “we support design.md” as a selling point, those competitor tools are basically promoting Google’s format for free. Google doesn’t even need to spend marketing budget — super cheap.
  • The training data flywheel. Once it’s open-sourced, people will put their own project’s design.md in public GitHub repos. Google can then use those files to train “AI that generates UI” — structured design Tokens plus human-written design rationale is honestly the perfect combo for AI training data. Future Gemini gets to use this public data to become stronger and get a better excuse to charge more per Token.
  • Stitch’s free marketing funnel. Once developers get used to design.md, when they need a “design tool that speaks design.md”, the first thing they think of is Stitch — because Stitch is the source.

So when big companies open-source things and it looks like they’re losing money, they’re often trading time for ecosystem. Chromium, Kubernetes, TensorFlow all played this game — on the surface it’s a community contribution, but in reality it’s using “free” to lock in the standard early.

What problem is design.md actually solving?

So… what problem does design.md actually solve? Basically just this:

Making sure the UI that AI generates uses the same colours, font sizes, and corner radii every time.

This is really, really important. I’m sure everyone Vibe Coding has run into this — you ask the AI to make a Button, and then…

  • The first time it gives you #3B82F6
  • The second time #2563EB
  • The third time straight up bg-blue-500

The screen looks fine, all blue. But they’re not actually the same blue, so your project ends up with a whole watercolour palette of “blues”. Font sizes have the same issue — sometimes 1.25rem, sometimes 20px, sometimes text-xl.

This kind of inconsistency makes the UI feel disjointed, which makes the user experience inconsistent, which hurts the brand.

So why does this happen? The main reason is you haven’t given the AI a “design system rules” document.

Someone might say: “Can’t I just put it in CLAUDE.md?”

You can. But you have to know that CLAUDE.md (and AGENTS.md) are “natural-language rules” for the AI — there’s no structured colour palette or Token system. After reading, the AI still has to guess “which hex is Primary” or “which corner radius should this button use”, and it ends up guessing in the end anyway.

So design.md is basically turning that “design system rules” document into a file that machines can parse precisely + humans can understand the reasoning behind.

So what does design.md actually look like?

We’ve kept saying “machines can parse precisely + humans can understand the reasoning behind”, so what does this file actually look like? Pretty simple — design.md is just a Markdown file.

The structure splits into two parts:

  • YAML Front Matter: the precise design Tokens (colours, typography, spacing, corner radius, components).
  • Markdown body: the design rationale — why this colour, when to use it, when not to use it.

By splitting the file this way, the AI doesn’t just know “Primary is #1A1C1E“ — it also knows “Primary is the deep ink used for headlines and core text”. So when it produces UI, it’s a lot less likely to use the wrong colour for a button or the wrong size for a piece of text.

YAML Token block

The YAML block looks roughly like this:

A few key points:

  • colors, typography, rounded, spacing, components are the five main Token categories.
  • Tokens can reference each other — for example, button-primary‘s backgroundColor is written as "{colors.tertiary}", so when you change the tertiary colour later, everything using it updates automatically.
  • Colours support any valid CSS colour string: hex, rgb, hsl, oklch — all good.

If you’ve used Tailwind’s tailwind.config.js or the W3C Design Tokens Format Module (DTCG), this’ll feel really familiar.

Markdown body block

After the closing --- of the YAML, you get the design reasoning written for humans (and AI) to read.

Here’s the officially recommended section order:

  • Overview
  • Colors
  • Typography
  • Layout
  • Elevation & Depth
  • Shapes
  • Components
  • Do’s and Don’ts

Only Colors (and the primary colour inside it) is required. The other sections are optional — write them only if your design system has that piece.

A concrete example — the Colors block might look like this:

This is basically telling the AI “this colour is called Tertiary, use it for primary action buttons, don’t use it for body text”.

Wait — there’s a CLI!?

Yep, design.md also ships with some really handy tools. Mostly they do four things:

  • lint
  • diff
  • export
  • spec

Installation and usage is super simple:

ps. If you’re on Windows, the .md extension might get eaten by file association, so you’ll need the designmd alias:

So let’s go through what each tool does.

lint: validate the DESIGN.md structure

I’m pretty sure lint will feel familiar to any developer — it’s the same concept as ESLint, Stylelint, Prettier.

In short, lint checks the following:

  • Whether the YAML schema is correct
  • Whether Token references are broken (e.g. you wrote {colors.tertiery}, missing an a?!)
  • Whether there’s a primary colour
  • Whether WCAG AA contrast passes (I think this one’s the killer feature)
  • Whether there are orphan Tokens that are defined but never used
  • Whether the section order matches the recommendation

For the WCAG AA rule, it’ll straight up tell you “on-secondary against secondary only hits X.X:1 contrast, doesn’t pass AA”. So accessibility checks are baked right into the design system file.

diff: compare two versions

This one is honestly pretty fun — just like git diff, you can compare two versions of DESIGN.md. Usage is simple:

It’ll tell you which Tokens changed, which were deleted, which were added.

The main use case: when you’re doing a version bump on your design system, swapping primary from #1A1C1E to #0A0A0A, and you want to know “how many components in the project are going to change colour because of this” — diff lists every affected Token so you can quickly see what needs updating.

export: convert to other formats

The export tool is genuinely impressive in my opinion, especially if you want to treat design.md as the “upstream” source and convert it into Tailwind / CSS / DTCG formats that the frontend actually uses.

FormatDescription
json-tailwindConverts to a Tailwind v3 config object
css-tailwindConverts to a Tailwind v4 @theme CSS block
dtcgConverts to the W3C Design Tokens Format Module

Usage:

The upside here is you only have to focus on design.md, then export to Tailwind / CSS / DTCG. Frontend, designers, and AI Coding tools all use the same source of truth.

(This one’s seriously powerful.)

spec: hand the spec to the AI

Last one is spec, also dead simple to use:

This command outputs design.md’s own format spec.

After that, you can hand the spec to your AI Coding tool of choice (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot) and tell it “please follow this spec when reading my DESIGN.md”. The AI then knows how to parse the YAML schema, that Token references use {path.to.token} syntax, what’s required, what’s optional.

Add --rules and it’ll output the linting rules as well.

So how do you get a DESIGN.md in the first place?

In case you’re wondering where DESIGN.md actually comes from, there are basically two ways:

  1. Write one by hand, following the YAML + Markdown structure above.
  2. Pick a style you like from VoltAgent/awesome-design-md, drop its DESIGN.md straight into your project root, then tell the AI:

Please generate UI following the spec in DESIGN.md

That’s it. Pretty simple, right?

How do you use it with AI Coding tools?

Now that we’ve covered design.md’s structure and CLI tools, let’s talk about how to use it with AI Coding tools.

Since design.md’s whole design goal is “turn the UI design system into something that can be continuously understood by AI”, the usage is pretty simple:

  1. Put DESIGN.md in your project root.
  2. In CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / .cursor/rules/*.mdc, add a line: “For UI-related tasks, please read DESIGN.md first and strictly follow the Tokens and design rationale inside.”

Yep, that’s it. Super easy.

One small reminder at the end, though, and it’s kind of an old chestnut at this point:

Don’t stuff design.md with irrelevant junk.

The whole design philosophy is to give the AI a “distilled” version of your design knowledge — same direction as Agent Skills that I shared before. Give the AI the key points, not everything.

Note
Right now, design.md doesn’t ship with official integration examples for Claude Code / Cursor / Copilot — only the CLI and TypeScript API. So you need to guide the AI to read design.md via your CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / rules file.

Wrap-up

Let me wrap this up.

So what exactly is design.md?

  • A design system document for AI Coding tools, in a “YAML Token + Markdown design rationale” format.
  • The pain point it solves is AI-generated UI having inconsistent colours, font sizes, and spacing.
  • The built-in CLI can lint (with WCAG contrast checks), diff, export to Tailwind / DTCG, and output a spec for the AI.

Hope this post helps you fix the “AI generates UI with inconsistent colours, font sizes, and spacing” pain so your AI Coding workflow goes a lot more smoothly.
entry.summary: design.md is a format spec that Google Labs incubated from their AI design tool Stitch and then open-sourced. The goal is to make AI Coding tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot actually understand your design system. This post covers what design.md is, how it's different from rules files like CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md, what the mixed YAML Token + Markdown rationale structure looks like, a real example (Heritage), and what the built-in CLI can do (lint with WCAG contrast checks, diff, export to Tailwind v3/v4 or W3C DTCG, spec) — plus how to wire it into your AI Coding tool of choice.

entry.title: Stats — The Free Open-Source macOS Menu Bar System Monitor I Use Every Day (CPU, GPU, Temperature, Network)
entry.content:
Intro

This post is about my favourite tool on macOS — Stats. I recently jumped from an M1 to an M5, and even though Apple’s migration tool moved everything over cleanly, Stats somehow refused to migrate. So it ended up being basically the first app I reinstalled on the new machine, which is a pretty good summary of how much I rely on it. This post covers what it does, how to install it, and a few of my own setup habits.

If you’ve read my earlier posts — How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl or Raycast Extensions I Actually Use — this one belongs in the same series. All of them are tools I’ve kept on every Mac for years.

So what is Stats?

Quick intro first.

Stats is a system monitor for macOS. Compared to Activity Monitor, it puts everything you’d want to glance at right where you can see it — I check Mac temperatures, network throughput, and memory use throughout the day without ever opening anything.

It’s an MIT-licensed open-source project, so it’s completely free, no feature gates, no ads, no upgrade nags. And you don’t really need to worry about it doing anything sketchy in the background — if it did, you wouldn’t be the only one to find out, since a lot of people use it.

Here’s the quick rundown of what it can monitor:

ModuleWhat it shows
CPUProcessor usage, per-core state, frequency
GPUGPU usage (Apple Silicon and external GPU both supported)
MemoryRAM usage, swap state
DiskPer-disk read/write speed, remaining space
SensorsSensor readings (CPU temp, GPU temp, voltage, power, etc.)
NetworkLive up/down throughput, public IP, Wi-Fi signal strength
BatteryBattery level, health, charge/discharge state
BluetoothBattery level of connected Bluetooth devices
ClockMultiple timezone clocks
RemoteRemote monitoring of other Macs

Pretty cool, right? Stats also has a Remote module. If you’ve got a Mac mini at home running OpenClaw or Hermes, this is genuinely useful — you can keep an eye on the remote machine straight from the menu bar instead of opening a remote desktop session.

I haven’t actually used Remote myself yet, but reading through the Stats site writeup it looks pretty nice.

Installing Stats

Enough preamble — how do you install it? My recommendation is still Homebrew.

A single command in the terminal:

Note
Stats is a GUI application, so you have to use --cask. brew install stats without --cask will fail, since Homebrew will go looking for a command-line formula by that name.

If you’d rather skip Homebrew, head to the GitHub Releases page and grab the latest Stats.dmg directly.

The modules I actually use

Going through each module one by one.

CPU module

CPU is one I leave on permanently. Being able to see live CPU usage means I can answer “what is this thing doing right now?” almost instantly — especially when the Mac suddenly feels sluggish. The first thing I want to know is whether the CPU is pinned.

GPU module

I turn this one on when I’m running an LLM locally. GPU usage spikes hard in that scenario, so a quick glance tells me whether the model is actually doing work or stuck somewhere mid-step.

Memory module

I watch memory closely too — once you run out, the system starts swapping to disk and everything slows to a crawl.

Note
One thing to mention — macOS handles memory differently from Windows. It’ll happily use unused RAM as cache, so you might see Memory used looking high while the system actually has plenty of free room.

Disk module

I rarely look at this one, so I usually keep it off — unless I’m doing something disk-heavy and want to see throughput.

Sensors module

The Sensors module is genuinely important to me — I’m mostly on an Air, and if I don’t watch temperatures the Mac will hit thermal throttling and everything stutters.

Stats reads temperatures straight into the menu bar:

  • Per-core CPU temperatures
  • GPU temperature
  • SSD temperature
  • Battery temperature
  • Various motherboard zones
  • Overall power draw

One thing worth noting — for this module the temperatures basically only show up in the menu bar.

Network module

Another one I leave on permanently. Whether the network’s working — and how fast it’s running — is one of the things I most want at a glance.

Note
Stats fetches your public IP by calling an external API. If that bothers you privacy-wise, you can disable it in the Network module settings.

Battery module

Hmm, this one’s pretty useful if you’re on an Intel-era Mac — Intel Macs have noticeably less stable battery behaviour and health compared to Apple Silicon, so I’d recommend keeping this one on if that’s you.

Bluetooth module

I barely touch this one. If your Bluetooth feels flaky, it’s worth opening up.

Clock module

I don’t really use this one either, but the convenience is that you can pin multiple timezones — handy if you’re collaborating across time zones (probably).

At most I pin a US West Coast time, mainly so I can check maintenance windows on overseas services.

Extra tip — desktop widgets

The menu bar gets crowded fast, so I move a few of the things I care about onto the desktop instead. Right-click the desktop → Edit Widgets:

Then find the widget you want and drag it onto the desktop:

This lets you keep more detail on the desktop itself — for me, it’s CPU, GPU, and memory usage.

Common questions

A couple of issues that come up.

Desktop widgets show “No data available”?

You need to enable the “macOS widgets” option for them to work. Stats’s desktop widgets are built on top of macOS’s native Widget framework, so if that option’s off, you’ll just see “No data available” forever.

Heads-up though — enabling this also makes Stats hammer disk writes pretty constantly. The maintainer’s tracking the issue: Widgets do not update or high disk writes.

Desktop widgets stuck on “Enable in Settings”?

This one seems to happen regularly with no obvious workaround — I’m just waiting on an upstream fix.

Wrap-up

Quick wrap-up.

The reasons I personally recommend Stats:

  • Open source, free, MIT-licensed
  • Modular — turn on what you want, leave the rest off
  • Clean interface, with localisation
  • Surfaces things macOS itself isn’t very forthcoming about, like CPU temperature

Hope this is useful.
entry.summary: A walkthrough of Stats — the open-source macOS menu bar system monitor I run on every Mac I own. Covers what it shows (CPU, GPU, RAM, disk, network, battery, sensor temperatures, Bluetooth, multi-timezone clock, plus a Remote module), how to install it with `brew install --cask stats`, a quick tour through each module, the desktop widget trick, and a couple of macOS 26 widget quirks worth knowing about.

entry.title: Make /llms.txt Return text/markdown on GitHub Pages: A Cloudflare Transform Rule Guide
entry.content:
Intro

I’ve written before about Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO and From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready’s AI Agent Readiness Test. Both touch on /llms.txt — basically an index file that tells AI search engines what good stuff your site has. And one of the key things is that you really need to serve it with Content-Type: text/markdown.

So I went ahead and dropped /llms.txt and /llms-full.txt onto my gh-pages site like a good boy, ran curl -I to double-check, and what came back was…

So this post is about how I used a Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule to flip that content-type: text/plain into text/markdown; charset=utf-8. No Worker required, the free plan covers it.

Why is /llms.txt coming back as text/plain?

The answer is pretty boring: because the file extension is .txt (yep, that’s literally it).

Every web server, when it serves a static file, looks up a table called the MIME type map to decide what Content-Type header to send. GitHub Pages uses mime-db, which is the industry-standard mapping table. The relevant entries look like this:

ExtensionMapped Content-Type
.txttext/plain
.mdtext/markdown
.htmltext/html
.xmlapplication/xml

So anything ending in .txt gets tagged as text/plain — even if you wrote markdown inside it, the web server doesn’t peek at the body. It only looks at the extension.

You might be wondering at this point: AI can still read text/plain just fine, right? The content is markdown either way, so what’s the big deal?

True — AI can definitely still read it. But there are two small problems:

  1. You lose the explicit format signal: when an AI crawler gets text/plain, it treats the content as plain text and won’t necessarily kick in its markdown parsing logic. Headings, lists, links — all that structure can end up being treated as flat prose, and you lose the actual benefit of writing in markdown.
  2. Some tools key off Content-Type: Anthropic and OpenAI both mention in their docs that sites serving markdown should label it as text/markdown. That way an AI Agent immediately knows “ah, this is clean markdown intended for me” and treats it accordingly.

So getting Content-Type right matters more than it sounds.

Why a Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule?

For a need this simple — “I just want to override Content-Type” — the Response Header Transform Rule is the cleanest tool for the job. On top of that, my domain is already on Cloudflare anyway (and gh-pages itself doesn’t give you anywhere to touch response headers).

So let me walk through how to set it up.

The setup

The whole thing takes maybe a few minutes.

Step 1: Get to the Transform Rules page

  1. Log into the Cloudflare Dashboard
  2. Pick the domain you want to set this up on (I’m using my blog israynotarray.com as the example)
  3. In the left sidebar, click Rules → Transform Rules
  4. Find the Response Header Transform Rules section and click Create rule on the right

Here you pick “Response Header Transform Rules”:

Step 2: Name the rule

I named mine Set markdown content-type so I can tell at a glance later what this rule does.

You could also call it llms.txt markdown header or whatever you prefer.

Step 3: Write the match condition

This is the part where things get a little tricky lol.

So my personal recommendation: just use the Edit expression mode and paste the whole thing in:

This expression does three things:

  • Matches /llms.txt
  • Matches /llms-full.txt
  • Matches anything ending in .md (if you’ve set up a “one .md per article” pattern)

After you hit Done, your screen should look like this:

Step 4: Set the header to inject

This is the most important step. Scroll down to the Then… section, click the Set a new header button, and three fields will expand for you to fill in:

  • Action: pick “Set static”
  • Header name: Content-Type
  • Value: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

I’d recommend keeping the charset=utf-8 part — without it, Chinese (or any non-ASCII) content can end up garbled on some clients.

Leave Place at on the default “Last”, hit Deploy on the bottom right, and you’re done.

Step 5: Verify with curl

Once deployed, open your terminal and run curl -I to inspect the response headers:

You should see something like:

If content-type is now text/markdown; charset=utf-8, the rule is live.

Note
If you’re still seeing text/plain, it’s probably Cloudflare’s edge cache hanging onto the old version. Either add -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" and re-curl, or go to the Caching page in the Cloudflare Dashboard and hit “Purge cache”.

Wrapping up

A few takeaways:

  1. On gh-pages, anything ending in .txt defaults to text/plain. When that’s your /llms.txt, AI crawlers don’t really treat it as markdown.
  2. A Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule fixes /llms.txt, /llms-full.txt, and any .md files all at once — and the free plan’s 10-rule allowance is more than enough.
  3. When writing the match condition, go with Edit expression mode — it skips the easy mistake of picking “Full URI” instead of “URI Path” in the dropdown.
  4. After deploying, curl -I is your friend for confirming the header rewrite actually took effect.

Once this rule’s in place, the next AI crawler that fetches /llms.txt will get a clear markdown signal.

If you want to go further and do actual Markdown Negotiation (where the AI sends Accept: text/markdown and you transform the entire HTML article into markdown on the fly), you’ll need to step up to a Cloudflare Worker — I covered the full Worker code for that in From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready’s AI Agent Readiness Test.

With this in place, the final piece of the AEO setup falls into place. Hope this post gives you a clearer picture of what Cloudflare Transform Rules can do.
entry.summary: I dropped /llms.txt onto my gh-pages site, ran curl -I, and found it coming back as text/plain. This post walks through fixing that with a single Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule — no Worker needed, free plan covers it. Covers why .txt defaults to text/plain via mime-db, the free plan's 10-rule allowance, the full Dashboard setup, how to write the match expression, and verifying with curl -I.

entry.title: Is AI Getting Quietly Dumber? AIStupidLevel: A 24-Hour Watchdog for LLM Degradation
entry.content:
Intro

You’ve probably hit this before — yesterday the AI felt sharp, fixed your bug without you even asking, and threw in a few extra cleanups along the way. Then today, same kind of problem, and suddenly it refuses to touch anything you didn’t explicitly point at, starts going in circles, or just answers something unrelated. And you start wondering “wait, is AI getting quietly dumber?”

I think a lot of people have had this feeling, but it’s hard to actually pin down. Is the provider messing with the model? Is today’s problem just genuinely harder? Or is it all in your head? Without a number to point at, it just stays an urban legend.

So this post is about a site called AIStupidLevel — the name pretty much spells out what it does. It runs round-the-clock tests against the major AI models and turns “is AI getting dumber” into an actual curve you can look at.

That’s enough of a hook to write about it.

So what is AIStupidLevel exactly?

In short, AIStupidLevel is a third-party benchmark platform that continuously monitors whether AI models are regressing. Think of it like a system health check, except it’s checking the “health” of AI models — flagging which ones have quietly started slipping. It runs 24/7 against 21 production AI models from 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM) and turns each model’s current performance into a score on a dashboard.

Some people might wonder if this is run by some AI monitoring company. It’s not — it’s an open source project. So it leans toward the “third-party watchdog” role, which I think matters here. You don’t want the people grading the models to also be selling them.

OK, with the basics out of the way, let’s keep going.

Hold on — why would an AI “get dumber”?

I think a lot of people would push back here:

Doesn’t the model get trained once and then frozen?

A few things are going on:

  1. Silent updates: If you’ve worked with the API, you’ve probably seen version strings like gpt-4.0-20240924 — that trailing date hints at a specific build. But when you pick a model inside Codex or similar tools, you don’t see that level of detail. Some models don’t even have versioned IDs, just a generic gpt-4. So you can’t actually tell which build you’re hitting, and a silent swap is really hard to notice.
  2. Quantization: To handle global peak traffic and save compute, providers sometimes compress the model (quantize it).
  3. Throttled compute: Once usage crosses a threshold, providers may cap how much compute each user can pull, which makes outputs feel worse.
  4. Compute migration: When a new model is about to launch, providers sometimes shift compute away from the older model onto the newer one, and the older one starts feeling sluggish. From my own time hitting the API, I run into this a lot — the same prompt suddenly degrades to “kind of dumb” overnight, then a few days later the provider ships a stronger version, and shortly after, the old version goes back to normal.

I covered quantization more thoroughly in my earlier post Want to Run an LLM Yourself? Understanding Model Parameters and Quantization So You Stop Picking the Wrong Model — short version, it’s a compression technique that saves resources but loses some detail.

So the takeaway:

An AI model isn’t actually a frozen thing. Providers can silently update it, quantize it, throttle it, or migrate compute away from it — and any of those will degrade what you experience. The hard part is, you usually can’t tell. That’s why “is the AI getting quietly dumber” is a real question, not paranoia.

How does it test?

Lots of words above, but I haven’t gotten to how AIStupidLevel actually tests. The core idea is simple:

  1. Fixed question bank, run repeatedly: It maintains a fixed set of tasks, throws them at each model on a schedule, and logs the scores.
  2. Same question N times: Because model outputs are stochastic, it runs each task N times and takes the median, plus a 95% confidence interval.

Sounds simple, right? The devil is in the details.

Same question N times, because AI is non-deterministic

“AI outputs are non-deterministic, right?”

Right — and the test design leans into that. If you only ran each task once, the confidence in the score would be really low. So each task gets run at least N times (and N is 5 in AIStupidLevel’s case), and the score is the median across runs. That way you’re tracking the model’s “typical performance” instead of getting fooled by a single lucky or unlucky roll.

Four test suites rotating in

AIStupidLevel doesn’t just run one kind of test — it has several different suites taking turns, each watching for different things. A table is probably clearest:

Test SuiteHow oftenWhat it tests
Speed test (coding)Every 4 hours147 coding problems, gauging overall coding ability
Deep reasoningDaily5–7 turn multi-turn dialogues, checking if long-conversation logic falls apart
Tool callingDailySpins up a real Docker sandbox so the AI can actually run multi-step flows like execute-command, read-file, write-file
Drift detection (canary)Hourly12 lightweight quick checks, first line of defense against anomalies

You can see the design: high-frequency quick scans plus low-frequency deep tests. The hourly canary plays sentinel — if something starts looking off, it sounds the alarm. The daily deep reasoning and tool-calling runs are the heavier full-body checkup.

The tool-calling suite is the one I find especially interesting. It actually spins up a Docker sandbox (think of it as an isolated mini-computer) and has the AI run real commands inside it, rather than just “verbally” claiming it can use tools. The results are way closer to what you actually feel when using AI to write code.

Scoring isn’t just right vs wrong

So how does a single task get scored? This is another place where it gets pretty detailed. Instead of just checking correctness, it scores across multiple dimensions.

For coding problems, AIStupidLevel currently weights across 9 dimensions: correctness (40%), complexity (20%), code quality (15%), stability (10%), efficiency (5%), edge case handling (3%), debugging (3%), output format (2%), and safety (2%). Correctness clearly dominates, but even if the answer runs, you’ll still lose points if it’s a mess, misses edge cases, or spews garbage formatting.

How does it catch the moment a model starts getting dumber?

Just having a score isn’t enough — the score naturally bounces around (because, as covered above, AI is non-deterministic). The real question is: is this drop a real regression, or just normal noise?

This is the technical core of the project. It uses an algorithm called CUSUM for this. CUSUM didn’t come from AI research — it’s an old quality-control method from manufacturing. The idea is to keep accumulating the gap between observed performance and the baseline. Once the accumulated gap crosses a threshold, you call it: this isn’t noise, something actually changed.

On top of CUSUM, it also runs statistical significance testing (checking whether the difference is statistically meaningful, p-value below 0.05) as a second pass to keep false alarms down.

The real win here: with this statistical machinery, it can detect a degradation within hours of it starting, rather than waiting until people are venting on social media that “AI got dumber.” The system has 29 different warning categories built in, covering all kinds of anomaly patterns.

So what does the score look like? (Stupid Meter)

You can see the dashboard the moment you open the site.

Each model has a live 0–100 score next to it — the higher, the better it’s performing right now, basically a “smartness gauge” for AI models. Next to the score is a status tag telling you which of four states the model is in: STABLE, VOLATILE (jittering), DEGRADED (already worse), or RECOVERING (climbing back up).

Beyond the current score, it also lays out each model’s historical curve, so you can compare scenarios like “this model was rock-solid last week, why is it jumping around this week.” Combined with the 29 warning categories I mentioned, it’s pretty quick to tell which models are stable right now and which are acting weird.

So in practice it feels more like a stock-trading dashboard — you’re not looking at a static ranking, you’re checking “right at this moment, which model is worth using.” If you’re still wrestling with which AI tool to pick, my earlier post Which AI Coding Tool Should You Pick in 2026? pairs well with this one.

A quick word on Smart Router

Beyond the monitoring side, AIStupidLevel also ships a feature called Smart Router, which I think is a pretty interesting extension of the project.

In short, it’s an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint (meaning code you wrote against OpenAI barely needs to change). You drop each provider’s API key into it (stored with AES-256 encryption), point your base URL at it, and it routes each request to whichever model is currently in the best shape based on its live monitoring.

It currently offers six routing strategies — you just set the model field in your API call to one of these and it picks dynamically:

  • auto-best: overall pick — whichever model has the best combined score right now
  • auto-coding: picks whichever model is best at coding right now
  • auto-reasoning: picks the strongest at reasoning
  • auto-creative: leans toward creative output
  • auto-cheapest: picks the cheapest model above the quality bar
  • auto-fastest: picks the fastest model above the quality bar

If you just pass auto, it uses whichever strategy you’ve set as your default. And if you pass a specific model name (like claude-opus-4-7), it pins that model directly and skips the router. So when a model quietly starts slipping and Smart Router catches it, it auto-routes traffic to a sibling model that’s still healthy. Pretty useful if you actually want to wire AI into a product.

Wrap-up

OK, lots of words — let’s wrap.

So what is AIStupidLevel?

  • It’s an independent, open source (MIT License) third-party benchmark site that monitors 21 production AI models across 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM) 24 hours a day
  • The method is fixed question bank, run repeatedly — each task runs 5 times, score is the median plus a 95% confidence interval, across four test suites (high-frequency canary + low-frequency deep tests) on a rotation
  • It uses CUSUM change-point detection plus statistical testing to catch a model quietly degrading within hours
  • It also ships Smart Router, which auto-routes API traffic to whichever model is in the best shape based on the live monitoring

Next time you feel like the AI suddenly got dumber, don’t jump straight to blaming yourself — pop this site open and you might actually find the evidence.
entry.summary: This post walks through AIStupidLevel — a third-party benchmark site that keeps a 24-hour eye on 21 production AI models across 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM). It throws coding, reasoning, and tool-calling tasks at them every few hours, runs each task 5 times to take the median with a 95% confidence interval, and uses the CUSUM change-point detection algorithm to catch when a model is quietly getting dumber. We'll cover why models degrade (silent updates, quantization), how the four test suites work, how to read the Stupid Meter, and the bundled Smart Router that auto-picks the best model for you.

entry.title: How Much of Your Post Does AI Search Actually Grab? Breaking Down Claude's WebSearch and WebFetch
entry.content:
Intro

A while back I wrote Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO — Getting Your Site Found by AI Search, and right after finishing it a thought hit me: when AI does a web search, how much of my blog does it actually grab? The whole article verbatim? The first 500 characters? Or does it bail after seeing just the title? So I dug into it a bit, and this post walks through Anthropic’s official web_search and web_fetch tool docs, runs a quick test against my own blog, and ends with what all this concretely means for how you should write posts and copy.

First things first — “search” and “fetch” are two different things

Before we go any further, there’s one thing I need to make crystal clear: when AI runs a query, “search” and “fetching the page body” are not the same operation. They’re two separate things, handled as two distinct stages.

  • Stage 1 (WebSearch): the AI takes your question and calls the WebSearch tool (which hits a search engine). What comes back is a list of search results — each entry has things like URL and title, but no page body.
  • Stage 2 (WebFetch): after looking at the search result list, the AI decides which entries are worth digging into, then fires a WebFetch request at each of those URLs that basically says “give me the body of this page.” That’s when the actual content gets pulled in.

Wondering why it doesn’t just grab the body during the search stage?

The biggest reason is Context (context window) limits. Think about it — if every search shoved 10 results’ worth of full bodies in, your usable Context would blow up fast, and then you’d start complaining the AI is dumb and forgets what you just asked it (because, well, the Context overflowed). That’s why it’s split into two stages: search first to get a “list”, then decide which entries from that list to actually fetch.

I drew up a flow diagram to make the timing easier to picture:

Now that the two-stage split makes sense, let’s look at what each stage actually pulls in.

What does WebSearch pull in?

Going straight to Anthropic’s official web_search tool docs — every search result entry has only four fields:

  • url: the page URL
  • title: the page title
  • page_age: when the page was last updated
  • encrypted_content: encrypted content, not for the AI to read the article — it’s for multi-turn conversation citations

Yep, that’s it. Four fields.

What the AI sees during the search stage (WebSearch) is “URL, title, last updated” — three pieces of human-readable info. No body content at all.

What if the AI cites your content? There’s a cap on that too:

Each web_search_result_location‘s cited_text is up to 150 characters of the cited content

In short: at most 150 characters of quoted text. And that’s just the API-level spec.

Claude Code’s built-in WebSearch shaves it down further. According to Mikhail Shilkov’s breakdown of Claude Code’s internal behavior, Claude Code even drops page_age and encrypted_content, keeping only title and url.

So basically… at the search stage the AI sees nothing more than one title and one URL from your site. That’s it.

What does WebFetch pull in?

Now for when the body actually gets pulled — Stage 2, WebFetch, as mentioned above.

Once the AI has the search results, if it decides to open up a few entries, it fires one WebFetch request per URL, and that’s when the full body comes back. How much of it?

This needs to be split into two layers, because the API and Claude Code work differently.

Note
When I say “API” here, I mean the Anthropic API’s web_fetch tool. “Claude Code” means the WebFetch feature built into Anthropic’s product. The two have different specs and flows.

API-level web_fetch

The Anthropic API’s web_fetch tool has a parameter called max_content_tokens that developers can set themselves — though the official docs use 100,000 tokens in their examples.

The docs also give a reference conversion:

Content sizeEstimated tokens
Average web page 10 KB~2,500 tokens
Large doc page 100 KB~25,000 tokens
Research paper PDF 500 KB~125,000 tokens

So a medium-length Chinese blog post in plain text is usually 1–2,000 tokens, way below the 100K ceiling. Truncation basically isn’t a concern unless you wrote a 50,000-character monster (like Dream of the Red Chamber or something).

One thing to note though: web_fetch’s citation works differently from web_search. It uses start_char_index / end_char_index to pick out a specific position in the article (although the docs don’t pin down a hard character limit).

Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch

Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch goes a different route.

Per Mikhail Shilkov’s breakdown, the WebFetch flow is:

  1. Convert HTML to Markdown using the Turndown package
  2. Extract the first 100 KB of plain text
  3. Pass that 100 KB to the Haiku 3.5 model
  4. Haiku summarizes the answer based on your prompt and returns the summary to the main model

The real kicker is step 3. You’ll notice the main model (the Claude model you’re actually using) never sees the page’s original text — it only sees the version Haiku summarized. Which means what your writing turns into by the time it reaches the main model is decided by how Haiku reads it, not by how much you wrote.

The citation has a limit too. The rule Mikhail extracted from Claude Code’s internal prompt is “Enforce a strict 125-character maximum for quotes from any source document” — so quotes max out at 125 characters.

You’re probably wondering — so how much is 100 KB of plain text? For Chinese, where each character is roughly 3 bytes, 100 KB fits 30,000+ characters; for English at 1 byte per character, that’s well over 100,000 characters. No regular blog post is going to hit that ceiling. So the real concern should be “how do I get Haiku to extract the parts I want to be quoted,” not “how much content am I feeding Haiku.”

Running a real test against my own blog

Enough theory — let’s actually test it.

I had Claude call the WebFetch tool and pointed it at my blog’s homepage (israynotarray.com) to grab the body.

How do you actually test this? You can lean on Hooks (article in Mandarin) to log every stage’s input and output, which makes it clear exactly what the AI pulled at each step.

Here’s my Hook config — this way every WebSearch and WebFetch I/O gets logged:

The prompt I used:

The actual Input was:

After it ran, the WebFetch result — the summary that came back — looked roughly like this:

See it? What the AI actually pulled from my blog is just this tiny under-1000-character summary. A whole website obviously contains way more than this — the rest was never seen by the AI. The Haiku middle layer reads the page and only extracts what it judges relevant to my prompt. If I opened the same page in a browser, I’d see a full grid of article cards plus a sidebar — but Haiku doesn’t ship the full grid back.

I also tried an older post with a deliberately broken URL path, and got this:

Even the content of your 404 page is invisible to the AI — WebFetch just reports back the 404 Not Found error and the AI has no way to see what your 404 page says. Which means if your site has any path issues, you’ve refactored URLs, or you only have frontend routing without real pages, the AI can’t pull anything.

Side note — this lines up with a caveat in Claude’s official docs:

The web fetch tool currently does not support websites dynamically rendered with JavaScript.

If your blog is built on a frontend SPA and the content is entirely rendered by JavaScript runtime, what the AI grabs might just be empty-shell HTML with no articles visible at all. Static generators (Hexo, Astro, Next.js in SSG mode) are relatively safe, since the build output is fully rendered HTML — the AI grabs and immediately sees content (welcome to the Hexo club).

Don’t forget the robots.txt layer

There’s one more important piece — whether the AI can pull your site has a major prerequisite: robots.txt.

AI crawlers basically split into two types: search-style (cite and link back to your site) and training-style (eat content to feed the model, not necessarily linking back). The common mapping looks like this:

CrawlerTypeBehavior
Claude-SearchBot / Claude-UserSearchReal-time fetch when Claude answers, cites back
ClaudeBotTrainingFetches content to feed Claude training
OAI-SearchBot / ChatGPT-UserSearchReal-time fetch when ChatGPT answers, cites back
GPTBotTrainingFetches content to feed GPT training
PerplexityBotSearchUsed by Perplexity engine, cites back
Google-ExtendedTrainingFor Gemini training
CCBotTrainingCommon Crawl public dataset

If you want to be cited by AI but don’t want your content used for training, the most common strategy is “allow search-style, block training-style.”

Here’s a robots.txt template you can copy-paste:

For more detail on writing robots.txt, you can check out my earlier post A quick guide to robots.txt (article in Mandarin). For a full Agent Readiness setup to score 100, see From 3 to 100! How to Get Your Site to Pass isitagentready’s AI Agent Readiness Check.

So what does this concretely mean for writing?

After all of that, what does this actually mean for how you write? It means a lot, actually. Once you understand the constraints above, there are four things you can specifically work on.

Titles need to stand on their own

At the search stage, all the AI sees about your article is two fields — title and URL.

If your title needs a subtitle or context to make sense, when the AI lines it up against ten other results it’ll get skipped.

A quick comparison:

  • Weaker: “Implementation Notes”
  • Stronger: “Complete Implementation Notes: Content Negotiation for HTML-to-Markdown in a Cloudflare Worker”

The stronger version packs in “topic, tool, what it does, and article type” — the AI doesn’t even need to open the page to know whether it’s worth fetching.

Lead with the answer

At the WebFetch stage, the Haiku middle layer reads top-down. The first 300–500 characters decide what it summarizes back. If your opening is “Before we get into X, let’s recap a bit of history…”, Haiku reads halfway through and discovers the intro is all background and no answer — so it just summarizes the background.

The right move is to make the first sentence of every H2 a direct conclusion, then add the context after. I covered this principle in Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO — Getting Your Site Found by AI Search too — worth reading alongside this.

Design single sentences that can be quoted standalone

cited_text is 150 characters on web_search and 125 characters on Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch. That means when the AI quotes you, the slot it has is one short sentence that “makes sense without context.”

Consciously design sentences like that. For example:

  • Weaker: “This is a bit different from the method mentioned earlier — the main difference is…” (makes no sense without context)
  • Stronger: “llms.txt was proposed by Answer.AI co-founder Jeremy Howard in 2024, with the goal of proactively telling AI what important content a site has.” (stands on its own without surrounding text)

After writing a paragraph, pick one sentence and ask yourself: if someone who hadn’t read the rest of the post saw just this sentence, would they get it? If yes, it has a shot at being quoted.

Use structure as Haiku’s navigation markers

H2, H3, - lists, tables, ```js code blocks — these Markdown structures are especially useful for the middleware summary layer. When Haiku reads the Markdown converted from your HTML, it treats headings as “what this section is about” indexes, lists as “main points” signals, and tables as “supporting data” units.

If your whole article is pure prose paragraphs, Haiku has no markers and has to grind through it semantically — what comes out is scattered. If you have clear structure, Haiku can summarize along the markers, and the result lines up with the points you actually want quoted.

Wrap-up

To bring it all together.

So how much of your blog does AI search actually pull?

The answer breaks down into three layers:

  • Search stage: title + URL only
  • Body fetch stage: the API default can fit your whole article, but Claude Code goes through Haiku summarization with a 100 KB cut-off
  • Citation stage: web_search is 150 characters, Claude Code WebFetch is 125 characters

Writing for AI search means targeting those three gates — it’s not about getting the AI to memorize your entire post.

Hopefully this gave you a more concrete sense of how much AI search actually pulls from your blog.

If your blog hasn’t set up AI bot routing yet, copy the robots.txt template above to get the basics in place — the rest is just content over time.
entry.summary: This post digs into how much of your blog AI actually pulls in when it does a web search. I went through Anthropic's official web_search and web_fetch tool specs, broke down the fact that the search stage only returns title + URL, the 150-character cited_text cap, and Claude Code's built-in WebFetch using Haiku 3.5 to summarize a 100 KB truncation, then ran a real test against my own blog. Wraps up with four actionable AEO writing tips: titles that carry their own context, lead with the answer, design single sentences that can be quoted standalone, and use structure as navigation markers.

entry.title: How to Use Gemini Omni Video Generation: 30 Prompt Templates for Reels, Product Demos, and Logo Reveals
entry.content:
Intro

Honestly, I’ve been pretty skeptical of AI generation for a while. Fingers, on-screen text, audio — these are the details AI has always tripped on, and the output usually screams “yeah, this is AI” instead of looking like something actually shot on camera.

But I spent some time with Gemini Omni recently and… the improvement is real. The overall picture quality is pretty impressive. Some small details still aren’t perfect, but it’s hit that fuzzy zone between “this is clearly AI” and “this could be real footage” — especially this clip I generated the other day:

A bunch of friends saw it and were honestly thrown — they thought I’d actually shot a promo for “Code Review of My Youth” myself ( not happening ). So I’d say Gemini Omni has made some real, tangible progress on the “looks shot” front.

If you’re not totally familiar with prompt structure or how to interact with an LLM, you can flip through my earlier post on LLM, RAG, Prompt — A Look at the Common GenAI Vocabulary first. This one will jump straight into Gemini Omni.

This post breaks into four parts: first clearing up a common mix-up (Gemini Omni in the Gemini App vs. Veo 3.1 on the developer side — not the same thing), then what makes Omni stronger than Veo, then how to actually write prompts that hold up, and finally 30 scenario-specific templates — IG Reels hooks, product demos, Logo Reveals, Cinematic B-roll, Before-After transitions, knowledge-deck animations, quote cards, and Lifestyle / travel / food cuts — all copy-paste ready.

First, let’s clear this up: video in the Gemini App — is it Omni or Veo?

A lot of people ask “where did Veo go?” — and honestly the confusion makes sense. Early 2026 the video generation inside the Gemini App actually was Veo 3.1. But after the May I/O it all got swapped over.

In short:

  • Gemini Omni is the new unified multimodal model Google launched at I/O 2026 (May). It’s now the main thing powering video generation inside the Gemini App. 10-second clips, 9:16 or 16:9, native synced audio, accepts a mix of text + image + video + audio as input.
  • Veo 3.1 / Veo 3.1 Lite didn’t go away — they just got reassigned. They’ve been pushed to the developer side and are mainly available through the Gemini API, Vertex AI, and Google AI Studio, for engineers building products against the API or running large batch generations.

If I had to put it in everyday terms — Gemini Omni is more like a fully automatic camera: you tell it what to shoot and it handles the aperture and shutter itself. Veo 3.1 is more like a manual camera: more control, but you have to know what you’re doing. For most people hopping into the Gemini App to generate a Reels clip, an IG short, or a Logo Reveal, Omni is enough.

What makes Gemini Omni strong?

For me personally, four things about Omni actually stand out: native synced audio, multimodal mixed input, Chinese text rendering finally being stable, and conversational editing. Walking through each one.

1. Native synced audio

The most painful thing about AI video used to be the audio, not the picture.

The picture would come out okay, but you’d have to hunt down voice / SFX / ambient separately on Pixabay or layer them in your editor afterwards.

Omni just swallows that part. You write things like “Dialogue: the female lead says ‘we have to leave now’”, “SFX: distant thunder”, “Ambient: low murmur of a late-night café” into your prompt, and it generates all three audio layers synced to the picture. Lip-sync lines up, SFX timing hits the right beats (yeah, sometimes things slip slightly, but it’s hard to tell now).

2. Multimodal mixed input

It’s not just text descriptions.

You can hand it a photo and say “make this photo come alive — coffee steaming, someone walking past the window”, and it’ll use that photo as the first frame and extend forward. You can also hand it an existing video and say “same scene but switch to dusk lighting” — that’s a style transfer. Image, audio, video, text can all be mixed in as input.

In my own case, I just handed Omni the cover of one of my books, and it dropped the cover straight into the video where the book was supposed to appear. That way I don’t have to re-describe what the cover looks like in every prompt, and it’s more accurate too.

3. Chinese text rendering is finally there

This is the same lineage as the leap Images 2.0 made on static images (if you haven’t seen what Images 2.0 can do on static images, take a quick look at How to Use GPT-5.5 and ChatGPT Images 2.0: 30 Prompt Templates for IG Cards, Knowledge Decks, and Product Mockups).

Omni brings that same capability over to motion. Chinese subtitles, opening titles, logo text in videos all come out correctly. Occasionally a character will still slip, but it’s hit the threshold of actually being usable.

4. Conversational editing

This is the one I personally find most useful. If a clip comes out wrong, you don’t have to rewrite the whole prompt — you can just say “change the lighting to warm dusk colors, keep everything else” in the next turn, and it’ll only touch that part. Really helpful for series content (same character, same style, different action) — you don’t have to re-describe every detail each time.

For me, the difference this round isn’t about “drawing more artistically” — it’s about “looking like it was actually shot”.

Post-production (pacing, captions, brand colors) still has to happen, but the “shooting” part has basically been eaten 80% by AI (probably?).

A universal structure for writing video prompts

Before you start copy-pasting templates, a quick note on the overall structure — otherwise you’ll just swap topics blindly and the output quality keeps bouncing around.

After working through the official prompt guide and a fair bit of trial-and-error, for the prompt to hold up these six dimensions all need to be described:

DimensionWhat it coversExamples
Composition / cameraHow the shot is framed, how the camera moveswide shot, close-up, dolly forward, orbit, POV, handheld
StyleThe overall aesthetic directioncinematic, IG vlog, anime, Pixar, documentary, studio
LightingLight source, color temperature, qualitygolden hour, neon, natural window light, soft studio, backlight
SceneLocation and background objectswooden table, café, Taipei street, minimalist white background
ActionWhat the subject is doing and how it moveswalking past, turning, reaching for a cup, hitting the keyboard
On-screen textText that appears on screensubtitle content, position, entrance animation

On top of those six, video has two extra dimensions that static images don’t — timing and audio. Those need their own slot:

ItemNotes
Timeline / TimestampFor multi-shot, use [00:00-00:03] to mark which segment happens when
AudioDialogue, SFX, Ambient — label all three layers separately

Note
Two things are outside your prompt’s control: aspect ratio (16:9 / 9:16) is selected in the Gemini App UI, and length is currently capped at 10 seconds by Google (their stated reason is a deliberate product decision, not a model limitation).
So you won’t see “please generate an X-second clip” in any of the 30 templates below. Pick the aspect ratio based on the scene — talking head and Reels-style go 9:16, cinematic and product demo go 16:9.

In short, don’t let the model guess. The more specific you are, the more precisely it generates.

Note
When you write Chinese dialogue, explicitly specify “clear Mandarin female voice (Taiwanese accent)” or “natural Mandarin male voice (Taiwanese)” in the prompt. Otherwise the voice occasionally drifts into an unintended accent or heavily English-leaning Mandarin — this is just a function of how the training data is distributed.

If you’re editing an existing video (local change instead of full regen), add a “preserve” vs “change” pair to your prompt:

The logic of this structure is “which parts of this clip do I want untouched, which parts do I want changed” — that way the model only modifies what you asked for and leaves the rest as-is.

30 recommended prompt templates

30 prompts organized into eight categories, all copy-paste ready.

For each one I’ll briefly explain the use case, then give the prompt body, and below that the video I actually got from running it. Swap whatever’s in [ ] brackets for your own topic.

Note
The example videos below were generated using the Chinese versions of these prompts (partly the point — to show off Omni’s Chinese rendering). The English templates here produce the same layouts with English text. Mirror the field names (“Composition:”, “Style:”, etc.) when you write your own.

Note
Video prompts work best in the 20–50 word range (or equivalent in Chinese) — too short and the model has nothing to go on, too long and the key points get diluted. I’ve tried to keep each template below in that range.

1. IG Reels / TikTok hooks

These are the clips that grab people in the first 2 seconds while they’re scrolling Reels. The key is to throw the conflict, question, or contrast out immediately — don’t waste time on setup.

1. Question-style hook (subject talking to camera)

The most direct opener — main character looking into the camera, asking a question that makes you want to keep watching.

2. Third-person narrator hook

No real face on screen — just hand-drawn or symbolic close-up with narration. Good for creators who don’t want to show their face.

3. Before / After two-second switch opener

Compress the contrast straight into the first two or three seconds — strong visual impact.

4. Countdown opener

A countdown naturally creates “what’s about to happen?” tension — great for listicle-style content.

5. POV scenario hook

First-person POV pulls viewers straight into the scene — maximum immersion.

2. Product / service demos

These are what I reach for most when making marketing assets, Side Project landing page heroes, or product intro videos. Key things: clean interface, tight pacing.

6. App UI walkthrough demo

Compress the most important user action into 8 seconds — good for IG ads or App Store previews.

7. SaaS Landing Hero animated background

The animated background for a Hero section, giving off “this product is sleek” energy.

8. Product mockup loop (360° rotation)

Standard for e-commerce product pages and IG product Reels — this one should be a seamless loop.

9. Service flow animation (explainer)

Turn an abstract service flow into something visual — good for the “How it works” section of a landing page.

3. Logo reveal / brand motion

Brand motion clips for the open or close of a video — 3 to 6 seconds is the sweet spot. Pacing has to be crisp, don’t let it drag.

10. Classic logo reveal

The safest version — works for pretty much any context.

11. Liquid metal logo reveal

Heavier-texture version — good fit for luxury brands or tech companies.

12. Hand-drawn ink logo

Good fit for indie/lifestyle brands or solo studios.

4. Cinematic B-roll

B-roll is the stuff you layer under your main footage to carry pacing. Omni is strong here — it understands cinematography language, so words like anamorphic, dolly forward, shallow depth of field all land.

13. City night drone shot

Great for vlog openers or social previews — instantly gives a sense of story.

14. Slow-motion raindrop close-up

The kind of shot you see in calming content, ASMR, or skincare ads.

15. Coffee brewing process

A go-to lifestyle B-roll — works for cafés, brand ads, indie vlogs.

16. Time-lapse city dusk

Time-lapse is a great fit for Omni — it can directly generate the kind of light-change content that’s inherently temporal.

5. Knowledge-deck animations

If you’ve made IG knowledge decks before, you know the format. Animating them usually bumps engagement another step up.

17. Concept card flip

Compress a term explanation into a “question on the front, answer on the back” flip animation.

18. Three-step flow animation

Animated version of “here’s how you do it, in three steps”.

19. Comparison table reveal

Two things side by side, revealed row by row — holds viewer attention better than a static graphic.

6. Before / After transitions

Contrast content tends to do well on Reels — people are wired to be interested in “change”.

20. Room makeover Before / After

Standard format for home, organization, glow-up content.

21. Code refactor Before / After

Engineering audiences eat this up — make the abstract result of a refactor visible.

22. Copy rewrite Before / After

Beloved by anyone doing copywriting / content teaching.

7. Quote card animations

Animated quote cards tend to hold attention longer than static images in a busy feed.

23. Quote card animation

Design / product / leadership quotes tend to get shared a lot.

Note
Gemini Omni blocks the likenesses and names of real public figures outright (politicians, tech founders, celebrities, athletes — same story across Veo / Sora / Midjourney and every other mainstream tool, since this is the standard platform policy against real-person likeness misuse). For quote / tribute style content, use a fictional name, anonymize, or just put your own name. In the example below I just used my own.

24. Short Chinese quote card

Japanese-minimal aesthetic — the kind of thing that gets bookmarked on IG.

25. Story-style page-flip quote

Two or three quotes in sequence, mimicking a page-flip — good for IG Story ads or brand storytelling.

8. Lifestyle / travel / food cuts

This kind of content tends to get the most consistent reach on IG Reels and TikTok — pacing and atmosphere matter most. Omni’s ambient audio really shines here.

26. Morning ritual vlog cut

A go-to opener for home / lifestyle vlogs.

27. Food plating close-up

Essential for recipe content, restaurant ads, food IG accounts.

28. One-day travel cut

A quick-cut day-in-the-life edit — a standard format for travel accounts.

29. Pet daily life close-up

Calming content — a go-to for cat / dog accounts.

30. City walk-and-talk

Common camera language for interviews, documentaries, podcast trailers.

Practical tips

Knowing the structure and templates isn’t quite enough — here are a few tips that have actually saved me time.

1. When Chinese fails (subtitles or accent)

If only a character or two in the subtitles slip, don’t regenerate the whole thing. Just go back to the chat in the Gemini App and say…

It’ll touch only that spot.

If the entire subtitle is garbled, it’s almost always because the prompt didn’t put the text in quotes — go back and wrap it in 「」 or 『』 and try again. Way more stable.

As for accent — Chinese dialogue occasionally drifts into an unintended accent or English-leaning Mandarin. Adding an explicit “clear Mandarin female voice (Taiwanese accent)” or “natural Mandarin male voice (Taiwanese)” to the prompt visibly improves the accuracy.

2. For seamless loop videos

For product mockups, logo backgrounds, SaaS hero motion — anything meant to loop endlessly — the prompt has to explicitly say “loops seamlessly” and “first and last frame should align”. The action also has to be periodic (rotate once, cycle through a motion, etc.) — don’t write something one-off like “a coffee cup gets knocked over”.

3. Aspect ratio per platform

Different platforms want different aspect ratios. Remember to set this in the Gemini App UI before generating (it only offers 16:9 and 9:16 — anything else you’ll have to crop after). Otherwise the output may not be directly usable. Common ratios:

Use caseRatio
IG Reels / TikTok / YouTube Shorts9:16 (portrait)
IG feed video1:1 (square) or 4:5 (portrait)
YouTube main video / ads16:9 (landscape)
Blog embed / OG animation preview16:9
Facebook feed1:1 or 16:9

You can see from this table that most short-form content is now 9:16 portrait. For Reels, set 9:16 in the App first; for YouTube main videos, set 16:9 — don’t generate everything at 16:9 and then crop to portrait, the framing gets thrown off.

4. Don’t cram too many actions into one shot

Honestly, 10 seconds is pretty limited.

If your prompt tries to do “walks through the door + sits down + opens laptop + starts typing + sips coffee” all at once, the model can’t fit it in, and usually drops motion smoothness or object consistency at the end.

A safe ceiling is 1–2 main actions per clip. If you need more, split using timestamp and use multiple shots — or just generate two separate clips and cut them together.

Wrap-up

So to wrap up — this post covers how Gemini Omni and Veo 3.1 split the work on the Gemini App side, what makes Omni strong, the prompt structure, and 30 scenario-specific prompt templates.

Honestly, before Omni I always thought AI video was a toy. Fine for concept demos or generative art, but if you wanted to actually use it for Reels, product demos, or Logo Reveals, there was always something off — janky motion, garbled text, or audio you had to source separately.

Omni is the first version that made me feel “okay, this is actually usable straight out of the box” (probably).

There are still limits, of course — anything that needs strict brand control or longer-form narrative still has to go back to real shoots and editing. But for 80% of the everyday short-form assets, these 30 templates will get you most of the way there.

If you want to turn Gemini into your own video generation helper, take a look at Gemini Gems Tutorial: Easily Build Your Own AI Assistant — you can package this prompt structure into a dedicated Gem so you don’t have to paste the whole thing every time.

If you’d rather skip the work entirely, you can also check out Awesome Gemini Gems, where I’ve collected a bunch of Gems I’ve prepared. I’ll probably add some video-generation Gems in the future too — drop by anytime.

Hope these 30 templates are actually useful for your content work.

As with every other post, if you paste these in directly and find the output isn’t quite the same — AI generation is just random like that (lol).
entry.summary: After Google launched Gemini Omni at I/O 2026 in May, video generation in the Gemini App got a generational swap — it replaced Veo 3.1, with 10-second clips, native synced audio, and Chinese text rendering all coming together. This post first clears up how Omni in the Gemini App and Veo 3.1 on the developer side actually split the work, then walks through what makes Omni strong and how to write prompts that hold up, and ends with 30 scenario-specific prompt templates — IG Reels hooks, product demos, Logo Reveals, Cinematic B-roll, Before-After transitions, quote cards, and Lifestyle / travel / food cuts — all copy-paste ready.

entry.title: Is Claude Code Auto Memory Hogging Your Memory? A Cleanup & Diagnostics Guide
entry.content:
Note
Auto Memory writes your feedback into ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/. It doesn’t use RAM, but it does eat Context Window and disk space. The good news is the memory files are usually just KB in size — the real reason ~/.claude/projects/ balloons to hundreds of MB or even GB is the conversation jsonl files sitting in the same folder. Claude Code uses cleanupPeriodDays (30 days by default) to auto-clean old session files, but if you just ran a massive session recently, waiting 30 days is a bit much. You can use find to fish out the big files instead.

Intro

A friend pinged me out of the blue the other day: “Hey Ray, my disk just lost 300–500 MB out of nowhere, and every time I launch Claude my Context drops way faster than usual. Any ideas?”

I had a pretty good guess. They’d been hammering Claude Code hard recently, so my first instinct was to check ~/.claude/projects/. Sure enough, that folder alone had eaten 300+ MB — and that’s tied to Claude Code’s Auto Memory mechanism. So this post is about that.

I also wrote a previous post — What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature — covering the basics and how Auto Memory works. This one picks up from there to talk about what Auto Memory actually “occupies,” what’s really eating your disk and Context Window, and how to deal with it.

What does Auto Memory actually occupy?

Quick answer first — Auto Memory occupies two things, and neither of them is RAM:

ResourceHow muchWhy
Context WindowFirst 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.mdAuto-loaded at the start of every conversation
Disk spaceUsually just KB-levelMemory content is written as Markdown files in your Home directory

Note
Heads up — when I say “memory” in this post I don’t mean RAM. I mean Context Window and disk space.

So how do you find this MEMORY.md? It’s just the index file inside your project’s memory/ folder, at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md. It lists all your memory files (Markdown files), one entry per line. Claude uses that index to find and read the corresponding memory content.

Context Window: how does MEMORY.md eat tokens?

Auto Memory content lives as Markdown files on disk. Every time you start a new conversation, Claude Code auto-loads the content of MEMORY.md into the Context Window so Claude can reference those memories during the conversation.

But MEMORY.md doesn’t get loaded in its entirety — there’s a limit:

Auto Memory only loads the first 200 lines or first 25KB (whichever comes first) of MEMORY.md into the Context Window.

In other words, if your MEMORY.md is over 200 lines or 25KB, the part past that threshold doesn’t get loaded into Context at all. This is also why some people swear Claude was supposed to remember something — they clearly told it, Claude even confirmed it remembered — but it has no recollection later. It just never saw that memory entry.

Here’s what an actual MEMORY.md looks like:

Each line is just a one-sentence index pointing at the actual memory file. With this kind of setup, even if your memory grows to dozens of entries, only these index lines get loaded into Context Window at startup. The actual detail behind each entry only gets read when Claude needs it in the conversation.

To make it concrete, the feedback_uiux_skill_for_ui_work.md mentioned above expands into something like this:

You’ll notice the name, description, type, originSessionId fields — the frontmatter format looks a lot like a Skill’s SKILL.md. You can think of Auto Memory as another flavor of Skill: Claude uses this metadata to figure out what kind of memory this is, which session it came from, and what it contains. That’s why the description field is critical.

If you ever find Claude can’t seem to recall a specific memory you thought it had, it’s probably because that file’s description isn’t clear enough — Claude can’t tell what the entry is about, so it doesn’t read it.

How to slim down MEMORY.md

If your MEMORY.md is approaching 200 lines, here are a few ways to trim it:

  1. Split details into Topic files: Keep MEMORY.md as just the index — one line per entry with a link, and put the actual content in separate .md files. Claude reads them on demand.
  2. Delete outdated memories: Just tell Claude “forget the previous rule about X” in the conversation, or manually delete the corresponding file under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/.
  3. Edit manually: Memory files are regular Markdown — open them in your editor and clean up.

Note
Most of the time you’d ask the AI to organize this for you rather than hand-editing — the whole point of Auto Memory is letting the AI manage these memories. Doing it manually kind of defeats the purpose. That said, understanding how the structure and loading mechanism work is still useful when something goes wrong and you need to debug it.

Disk space: the real culprit isn’t Auto Memory

Now the disk side — this is where most people misunderstand Auto Memory.

Let’s first see how much Auto Memory itself actually takes up. Using one of my own projects as an example, the memory folder looks like this:

The entire memory/ folder adds up to 52KB. At this scale, you’ll basically never need to clean it.

Now what about the whole projects/ folder?

Surprised? 339 MB! So who’s actually taking up all that space?

The answer is conversation jsonl — Claude Code saves every single conversation as a .jsonl file, where each line is a JSON object containing your prompt, Claude’s response, tool calls, tool results — everything written down. For long conversations or sessions with heavy file reads/writes, a single file can easily hit MB-level.

See for yourself:

The biggest single conversation file is already up to 8 MB.

So next time you notice ~/.claude/projects/ growing, don’t blame Auto Memory — run du and you’ll see the actual culprit is these .jsonl files.

So how do I auto-clean these? Meet cleanupPeriodDays

OK, good news — there’s a way to handle this. Claude Code has a built-in auto-cleanup mechanism that deletes session files older than 30 days when it starts up. The setting is called cleanupPeriodDays.

Note
